Symptoms: high body temperature above 38 degrees Celsius sweating chills shivering hot skin flushed face headache muscle aches fatigue loss of appetite dehydration rapid heartbeat
FirstAidSteps: Rest in a cool comfortable room; Drink plenty of fluids (water, clear broths, juices); Apply cool damp cloth to forehead; Remove excess clothing; Take fever-reducing medicine; Monitor temperature every 30–60 minutes
Medicines: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) 500–1000mg, Ibuprofen 400mg, Cool compress, Thermometer, Oral rehydration fluids
EmergencyTriggers: Fever above 39.4°C (103°F) lasting more than 3 days, Fever with severe headache or stiff neck, Fever with rash, Difficulty breathing
Warnings: Do NOT give Aspirin to children under 16. Do NOT over-bundle the patient. Febrile convulsions in children require immediate emergency care.

Symptoms: elevated temperature above 38 degrees in child hot forehead flushed cheeks irritability crying restlessness loss of appetite sweating shivering rapid breathing febrile seizure risk
FirstAidSteps: Keep child calm and resting; Give plenty of fluids (water, juice, oral rehydration solution); Dress in light clothing; Apply cool damp cloth to forehead and wrists; Give age-appropriate fever reducer; Monitor temperature regularly
Medicines: Children's Paracetamol (syrup/suppository), Children's Ibuprofen (age 6+ months), Thermometer, ORS
EmergencyTriggers: Fever above 38°C in infants under 3 months, Fever above 39°C in children 3–6 months, Fever lasting more than 5 days, Child is unresponsive or hard to wake
Warnings: NEVER give Aspirin to children — risk of Reye's syndrome. Dosage must be weight-based. Always consult a doctor for infants under 3 months.

Symptoms: red skin redness superficial burn pain on touch dry skin no blisters mild swelling heat exposure sunburn scalding warm skin surface burn localized pain
FirstAidSteps: Remove from heat source immediately; Cool the burn under cool (not cold/ice) running water for at least 10–20 minutes; Remove jewelry or tight items near the burn; Cover loosely with sterile non-stick dressing; Take pain relief if needed; Do NOT burst any blisters
Medicines: Burn gel / cooling gel, Sterile non-adhesive dressing, Paracetamol or Ibuprofen for pain, Aloe vera gel (soothing)
EmergencyTriggers: Burns larger than 3 inches, Burns on face/hands/feet/genitals/major joints, Blistering over large area, Suspected deep burn (no pain felt)
Warnings: Do NOT use ice — it causes further tissue damage. Do NOT use toothpaste, butter, or oil. Do NOT break blisters. Do NOT use fluffy cotton wool directly on burn.

Symptoms: large blisters deep tissue burn charred black skin white leathery skin severe pain or no pain due to nerve damage extensive redness swelling fluid oozing full thickness wound
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Cool burn with cool running water for 20 minutes; Do NOT remove clothing stuck to the burn; Cover loosely with cling film or sterile dressing; Keep the patient warm to avoid shock; Monitor breathing and consciousness
Medicines: Cling film (covering only), Sterile dressings, IV fluids (hospital), Pain relief (hospital-administered)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — call 999/911/112 immediately
Warnings: Do NOT use ice, creams, or ointments on severe burns. Do NOT remove clothing stuck to the skin. Third-degree burns may be painless due to nerve destruction.

Symptoms: skin contact with acid or alkali burning sensation skin discoloration blistering chemical splash corrosive damage redness peeling tissue necrosis eye irritation if splashed
FirstAidSteps: Ensure safety — wear gloves before helping; Remove contaminated clothing/jewelry; Flush affected area with large amounts of cool running water for 20–30 minutes; Do NOT try to neutralize the chemical; Cover loosely with sterile dressing; Call emergency services
Medicines: Sterile dressings, Eye wash (if eyes affected), IV fluids (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Always call emergency services immediately
Warnings: Do NOT apply creams or bandages tightly. Do NOT attempt to neutralize acid with alkali or vice versa. For eye exposure, irrigate eyes continuously.

Symptoms: entry and exit burn marks electrical contact muscle spasm loss of consciousness cardiac arrhythmia tingling numbness singed skin unconsciousness respiratory arrest internal tissue damage
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT touch the person if they are still in contact with the electrical source; Turn off the power supply at the source; Call emergency services immediately; If safe, begin CPR if person is unconscious and not breathing; Cover burns with sterile dressing; Treat for shock
Medicines: Sterile dressings, AED (if cardiac arrest), CPR equipment
EmergencyTriggers: Always call emergency services — electrical injuries can cause internal damage not visible externally
Warnings: Electrical burns may look minor but cause severe internal damage. Always seek emergency medical care. Risk of cardiac arrhythmia.

Symptoms: superficial skin cut laceration bleeding scrape abrasion minor bleeding sharp object wound skin broken redness around wound mild pain stinging sensation
FirstAidSteps: Wash hands before treating; Apply gentle pressure with clean cloth or sterile gauze to stop bleeding; Clean the wound under running clean water for 5–10 minutes; Remove visible debris gently — do NOT probe deep wounds; Apply antiseptic solution or cream; Cover with sterile adhesive bandage or dressing; Change dressing daily
Medicines: Antiseptic solution (Betadine/Povidone-Iodine), Antiseptic wipes, Sterile gauze pads, Adhesive bandages (plasters), Antibiotic cream (Neosporin/Bacitracin), Medical tape
EmergencyTriggers: Wound will not stop bleeding after 10 minutes of pressure, Wound is deep / has jagged edges, Wound is on the face, Wound caused by animal/human bite, Signs of infection (redness, pus, swelling, warmth)
Warnings: Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide inside a wound — it damages tissue. Tetanus booster may be needed for deep/dirty wounds. Seek medical care for puncture wounds.

Symptoms: deep wound heavy uncontrolled bleeding gaping edges exposed tissue tendon or bone visible arterial bright red pulsing blood rapid blood loss pale sweating shock signs
FirstAidSteps: Apply firm direct pressure with clean cloth/gauze — do NOT lift to check frequently; If blood soaks through, add more gauze on top; Elevate the injured limb above heart level; Apply pressure bandage; Do NOT remove embedded objects; Call emergency services or transport to hospital; Treat for shock if needed
Medicines: Sterile gauze, Pressure bandage, Tourniquet (for limb bleeding only — last resort), IV fluids (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Bleeding does not stop after 10–15 minutes of pressure, Wound is very deep, Suspected severed artery (bright red pulsing blood), Wound with embedded object
Warnings: Do NOT remove embedded objects. Do NOT use a tourniquet unless bleeding is life-threatening and uncontrollable. Tourniquets should be noted with time of application.

Symptoms: blood flowing from one or both nostrils nasal bleeding anterior epistaxis head tilt urge to swallow blood blood dripping from nose recent trauma or dryness
FirstAidSteps: Have the person sit upright and lean slightly forward (NOT backward); Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly (just below the bony bridge); Breathe through the mouth; Hold for 10–15 minutes without releasing to check; Apply cold compress to the bridge of the nose; Do NOT tilt head back; Do NOT blow nose for several hours after
Medicines: Cold compress / ice pack (wrapped in cloth), Saline nasal spray (for dry nasal passages), Nasal decongestant spray (short-term only)
EmergencyTriggers: Nosebleed lasts more than 20–30 minutes, Caused by head injury, Blood loss is large, Person on blood-thinning medication
Warnings: Do NOT tilt head backward — blood may flow into throat and cause vomiting. Do NOT stuff nose with tissue paper — it may damage fragile vessels.

Symptoms: itchy skin hives rash redness sneezing runny nose watery eyes mild swelling urticaria allergic contact dermatitis food allergy trigger seasonal allergy skin reaction
FirstAidSteps: Identify and remove/avoid the allergen trigger immediately; Wash affected skin with soap and water if contact allergy; Apply cold compress to reduce itching/swelling; Take oral antihistamine; Monitor symptoms for worsening; Avoid scratching
Medicines: Oral antihistamine (Cetirizine / Loratadine / Diphenhydramine / Chlorpheniramine), Hydrocortisone cream (1%) for skin reactions, Calamine lotion, Cold compress
EmergencyTriggers: Symptoms worsen rapidly, Swelling of lips/tongue/throat, Difficulty breathing, Signs of anaphylaxis
Warnings: Drowsiness is a common side effect of first-generation antihistamines (e.g., Diphenhydramine). Do NOT drive after taking them. Keep a record of the allergen.

Symptoms: throat swelling tongue swelling difficulty breathing wheezing hives all over body rapid pulse drop in blood pressure dizziness loss of consciousness anaphylactic shock severe allergic emergency bee sting food drug trigger
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Use epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen) into outer thigh; Lay the person flat — legs elevated (unless breathing difficulty, then semi-upright); Give a second dose of epinephrine after 5–15 minutes if no improvement; Begin CPR if person stops breathing; Stay with the person until help arrives
Medicines: Epinephrine auto-injector (EpiPen / Auvi-Q), Antihistamine (secondary, after epinephrine), IV antihistamines/corticosteroids (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — this is a medical emergency
Warnings: Antihistamines alone are NOT sufficient for anaphylaxis. Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line treatment. Even after improvement, the patient must go to hospital — symptoms can return (biphasic reaction).

Symptoms: frequent loose watery stools abdominal cramping bloating nausea urgency to defecate dehydration dry mouth sunken eyes dark urine weakness electrolyte loss gastroenteritis food poisoning
FirstAidSteps: Encourage increased fluid intake to prevent dehydration; Prepare and give Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS); Eat bland foods: bananas, rice, applesauce, toast (BRAT diet); Avoid dairy, fatty foods, and caffeine; Rest adequately; Wash hands thoroughly after each bathroom visit; For adults, consider anti-diarrheal medication if no fever
Medicines: Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS / Pedialyte), Loperamide (Imodium — adults only, not if fever present), Probiotics (Lactobacillus), Zinc supplements (children), Clear fluids (water, coconut water, broth)
EmergencyTriggers: Diarrhea lasting more than 2 days in adults / 24 hours in infants, Blood or mucus in stool, Signs of severe dehydration, High fever alongside diarrhea
Warnings: Do NOT give Loperamide to children under 12 or when there is bloody stool/fever — it can worsen bacterial infections. Keep breastfeeding infants on breast milk.

Symptoms: forceful stomach contents expelled through mouth nausea retching abdominal pain dehydration dry mouth weakness dizziness pale skin food poisoning gastritis viral illness motion sickness
FirstAidSteps: Rest and avoid solid foods for several hours; Sip small amounts of clear fluids frequently; Once vomiting stops, gradually reintroduce bland foods; Avoid dairy, spicy, fatty foods; Keep the person upright or on their side if drowsy to prevent choking; Give ORS if signs of dehydration appear
Medicines: Oral Rehydration Solution (ORS), Antiemetics (Domperidone / Metoclopramide — prescription), Ginger tea or candies (natural remedy), Ice chips to suck on
EmergencyTriggers: Vomiting lasts more than 24 hours, Blood in vomit, Signs of severe dehydration, Accompanied by severe headache or stiff neck, Vomiting after head injury
Warnings: Do NOT give solid foods until vomiting has stopped for at least 2 hours. If vomiting after head injury, always seek emergency care — it may indicate concussion.

Symptoms: dull constant pressure around forehead temples tightness band around head neck stiffness eye strain stress dehydration fatigue mild to moderate pain bilateral headache
FirstAidSteps: Rest in a quiet, dark room; Drink adequate fluids — dehydration is a common cause; Apply cold or warm compress to forehead or neck; Gently massage temples and neck muscles; Take over-the-counter pain reliever if needed; Avoid screen time; Practice relaxation techniques / deep breathing
Medicines: Paracetamol (Acetaminophen) 500–1000mg, Ibuprofen 400mg, Aspirin (adults only), Caffeine (small amount), Peppermint oil (topical)
EmergencyTriggers: Sudden severe 'thunderclap' headache, Headache after head injury, Headache with stiff neck + fever + rash, Headache with vision changes, Headache with confusion or weakness
Warnings: 'Thunderclap' headache (worst headache of life) may indicate subarachnoid hemorrhage — call emergency services immediately. Do NOT over-use pain relievers — can cause rebound headaches.

Symptoms: severe throbbing one-sided headache nausea vomiting light sensitivity sound sensitivity aura visual disturbances zigzag lines blind spot pulsating pain hours to days duration
FirstAidSteps: Move to a quiet, dark room immediately; Apply cold pack to forehead or neck; Take migraine medication at onset; Stay hydrated; Avoid triggers (bright lights, strong smells, noise); Try to sleep; Avoid screen time
Medicines: Triptans (Sumatriptan — prescription), Ibuprofen 400–600mg, Paracetamol, Antiemetics (if nausea present), Ergotamines (prescription)
EmergencyTriggers: Migraine lasting more than 72 hours, First-ever severe headache, Headache with numbness/weakness/speech difficulty, Headache with fever + stiff neck
Warnings: Triptans should not be used in people with cardiovascular disease. Medication overuse (more than 10 days/month) can worsen migraines. Always discuss with a doctor.

Symptoms: localized redness swelling itching stinging sharp pain small puncture mark raised welt hive at bite site bee sting wasp ant mosquito spider bite mild inflammation
FirstAidSteps: Remove the stinger if present — scrape sideways with a card/fingernail, do NOT use tweezers (may squeeze more venom); Wash the area with soap and water; Apply cold compress or ice pack (wrapped in cloth) for 10 minutes; Elevate the affected limb; Apply antihistamine cream or calamine lotion to reduce itching; Take oral antihistamine if itching is severe; Do NOT scratch
Medicines: Antihistamine cream (Hydrocortisone 1%), Calamine lotion, Oral antihistamine (Cetirizine/Loratadine), Ice pack, Antiseptic wipes, Paracetamol or Ibuprofen (for pain)
EmergencyTriggers: Signs of allergic reaction or anaphylaxis (swelling, difficulty breathing), Bee/wasp sting in mouth or throat, Multiple stings (risk of toxic reaction), Embedded tick
Warnings: Do NOT use tweezers to remove bee stingers — it squeezes the venom sac. Watch for signs of Lyme disease after tick bites (bullseye rash). Remove ticks with fine-point tweezers close to skin.

Symptoms: two puncture fang marks localized pain swelling bruising discoloration nausea vomiting blurred vision difficulty breathing drooping eyelids paralysis tissue necrosis neurotoxic or hemotoxic symptoms
FirstAidSteps: Keep the person calm and still — movement increases venom spread; Call emergency services immediately; Remove tight clothing/jewelry near the bite; Keep the bitten limb below heart level; Mark the time of the bite and symptoms; Note appearance of the snake if possible (do NOT try to catch it); Lay the person down if feeling faint
Medicines: Antivenom (hospital-administered), IV fluids (hospital), Pain relief (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — all suspected venomous snake bites require emergency care
Warnings: Do NOT cut the bite or try to suck out venom — this is ineffective and dangerous. Do NOT apply tourniquet or ice. Do NOT apply electric shock. Do NOT give the person alcohol.

Symptoms: puncture wounds torn skin bleeding redness around wound pain swelling risk of rabies tetanus infection deep tooth marks animal saliva contamination wound infection signs
FirstAidSteps: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and running water for at least 5 minutes; Apply gentle pressure to control bleeding; Apply antiseptic solution; Cover with sterile dressing; Seek medical attention — may need antibiotics and tetanus/rabies vaccination; Record details about the animal (vaccination status if possible)
Medicines: Antiseptic solution (Povidone-Iodine), Sterile gauze / bandage, Antibiotics (prescription — Amoxicillin-Clavulanate), Tetanus toxoid injection (if not up to date), Rabies post-exposure prophylaxis (if indicated)
EmergencyTriggers: All animal bites, especially from wild/stray animals, Deep puncture wounds, If animal suspected of having rabies, Bite on face/head/hands/feet
Warnings: Rabies is fatal if not treated promptly — do NOT delay medical attention for animal bites. All bites from stray or wild animals require rabies assessment. Cat bites are at high risk of infection.

Symptoms: tooth marks on skin skin broken bleeding high infection risk redness swelling warmth risk of hepatitis HIV transmission oral bacteria contamination wound showing signs of infection
FirstAidSteps: Wash the wound thoroughly with soap and clean running water for 5 minutes; Apply antiseptic solution; Cover with sterile dressing; Seek medical attention — risk of serious infection; Do NOT close the wound with tape if deep
Medicines: Antiseptic solution, Sterile dressing, Antibiotics (prescription — Amoxicillin-Clavulanate), Tetanus toxoid (if needed)
EmergencyTriggers: All human bites that break the skin — always seek medical care
Warnings: Human bites carry high infection risk from oral bacteria. Deep human bites may transmit hepatitis B/C and HIV — medical assessment is essential.

Symptoms: sudden inability to speak breathe or cough silent choking hands clutching throat panicked expression turning blue cyanotic face reddened before cyanosis food or object lodged in airway
FirstAidSteps: Ask: 'Are you choking?' — if they can cough, encourage coughing; Give up to 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand; Check mouth — if obstruction visible, remove it carefully; If back blows fail, give up to 5 abdominal thrusts (Heimlich maneuver): stand behind person, place fist above navel, cover with other hand, thrust sharply inward and upward; Alternate 5 back blows and 5 abdominal thrusts; If person loses consciousness, begin CPR and call emergency services
Medicines: No medicines required — this is a physical intervention only
EmergencyTriggers: If person becomes unconscious — call 999/911/112 immediately, Obstruction cannot be cleared after repeated attempts
Warnings: Do NOT perform blind finger sweeps in the mouth. Do NOT perform abdominal thrusts on infants — use back blows and chest thrusts only. Abdominal thrusts can cause internal injury — always seek medical evaluation afterward.

Symptoms: sudden silence inability to cry weak ineffective cough turning blue cyanosis around lips no air movement bluish face and lips foreign object food lodged in infant airway respiratory distress
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Hold infant face-down on forearm, support head; Give 5 firm back blows between shoulder blades with heel of hand; Turn infant face-up; give 5 chest thrusts (2 fingers on center of chest, just below nipple line); Alternate 5 back blows and 5 chest thrusts; If infant becomes unconscious, begin infant CPR
Medicines: No medicines — physical intervention only
EmergencyTriggers: Always — infant choking is always an emergency
Warnings: NEVER perform abdominal thrusts on infants. Hold infant at downward angle for back blows. Shout for help immediately.

Symptoms: sudden collapse unresponsive no breathing no pulse unconscious no sign of life skin ashen or blue lips cyanotic no reaction to stimulation sudden death witnessed collapse
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Ensure scene is safe; Begin CPR: 30 chest compressions at 100–120 per minute (compress 5–6 cm deep on adults); Give 2 rescue breaths after every 30 compressions (trained rescuers); If untrained, perform hands-only CPR (continuous compressions); Use AED (Automated External Defibrillator) as soon as available — follow voice instructions; Continue until emergency services arrive or person recovers
Medicines: AED (Automated External Defibrillator), No medicines in pre-hospital setting
EmergencyTriggers: Always — call 999/911/112 immediately
Warnings: Hands-only CPR (no rescue breaths) is effective for untrained bystanders. Do NOT stop CPR unless person shows signs of life, you are physically unable to continue, or qualified help takes over. AEDs are safe and easy to use.

Symptoms: severe chest pain pressure tightness crushing sensation radiating to left arm jaw neck back shortness of breath sweating nausea dizziness anxiety feeling of impending doom pale skin
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Have the person sit or lie down in a comfortable position; Loosen tight clothing; If conscious and not allergic, give one adult aspirin (300mg) to chew — NOT swallow whole; Monitor breathing and consciousness; Prepare to begin CPR if person becomes unconscious; Reassure and keep calm
Medicines: Aspirin 300mg (chew — NOT for those allergic or with recent GI bleeding), GTN (glyceryl trinitrate) spray/tablet if prescribed by doctor
EmergencyTriggers: Always — suspected heart attack is always an emergency
Warnings: Symptoms: chest pain/pressure, pain radiating to arm/jaw/back, shortness of breath, sweating, nausea. Do NOT give Aspirin if person is allergic or under 16. Aspirin is for heart attack — NOT stroke.

Symptoms: sudden face drooping arm weakness speech slurred sudden confusion trouble walking loss of balance severe sudden headache vision loss in one or both eyes facial asymmetry FAST signs
FirstAidSteps: Use the FAST test: Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech difficulty, Time to call emergency; Call emergency services immediately; Keep person calm and still; Do NOT give food or water — swallowing may be affected; Note the time symptoms started; If unconscious, place in recovery position; Begin CPR if they stop breathing
Medicines: No medicines in pre-hospital setting — hospital will administer thrombolytics if appropriate
EmergencyTriggers: Always — stroke is always a medical emergency. Time is brain.
Warnings: Do NOT give Aspirin for stroke — if the stroke is hemorrhagic (bleeding), Aspirin will make it worse. 'Time is brain' — every minute counts. Record the exact time symptoms started.

Symptoms: shakiness trembling sweating rapid heartbeat palpitations dizziness hunger confusion irritability pale skin blurred vision weakness fainting low blood glucose below 3.9 mmol/L
FirstAidSteps: Recognize symptoms: shakiness, sweating, confusion, paleness, dizziness, fast heartbeat; If conscious: Give 15–20g of fast-acting sugar — 4 glucose tablets, half cup of fruit juice, or regular (non-diet) soda, or 3–4 teaspoons of sugar; Rest for 15 minutes, then recheck blood sugar; If no improvement, repeat sugar dose; Once improved, give a snack with complex carbs (crackers, bread); If unconscious — do NOT give anything by mouth; call emergency services
Medicines: Glucose tablets (dextrose), Glucagon injection kit (for unconscious patients — prescription), Fruit juice / regular soda / sugar, Glucose gel
EmergencyTriggers: Person is unconscious or cannot swallow, No improvement after two doses of sugar, Blood sugar remains below 3.9 mmol/L (70 mg/dL)
Warnings: Do NOT give food or liquid to an unconscious person — risk of choking/aspiration. If glucagon is available and person is unconscious, administer it. Place unconscious person in recovery position.

Symptoms: excessive thirst frequent urination fatigue blurred vision headache slow healing wounds fruity breath acetone smell nausea abdominal pain high blood glucose above 11 mmol/L
FirstAidSteps: Have person drink plenty of water (if conscious) to help flush excess glucose; If person takes insulin — they may need to take their prescribed dose (consult their care plan); Encourage gentle activity if blood sugar is mildly elevated; Monitor blood sugar levels; Check for signs of diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): fruity breath, deep labored breathing, confusion
Medicines: Insulin (if prescribed — patient-specific), Water / fluids
EmergencyTriggers: Blood sugar above 13.9 mmol/L (250 mg/dL) and worsening, Signs of DKA, Fruity odor on breath, Confusion or loss of consciousness, Vomiting
Warnings: Do NOT adjust insulin dose without medical guidance. DKA is a medical emergency. If unsure whether hypoglycemia or hyperglycemia — if conscious, giving sugar is safer than withholding.

Symptoms: water in lungs coughing choking unconscious in water no breathing blue lips cyanosis altered consciousness after water immersion vomiting after rescue respiratory distress secondary drowning risk
FirstAidSteps: Ensure your own safety before entering water; Call emergency services immediately; Remove person from water carefully; Check for breathing and pulse; If not breathing, begin CPR immediately: 5 initial rescue breaths, then standard CPR (30:2); Keep the person warm — dry clothing, blanket; Even if person recovers, always seek emergency medical care
Medicines: CPR equipment, Warming blanket, Oxygen (emergency services)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — all near-drowning incidents require emergency care and hospital evaluation
Warnings: Secondary drowning can occur hours after the incident — always go to hospital even if person seems fine. Do NOT attempt to remove water from the lungs — focus on ventilation. Hypothermia may accompany drowning.

Symptoms: body temperature above 40 degrees Celsius hot dry skin no sweating confusion agitation slurred speech loss of consciousness rapid strong pulse flushed red skin heat emergency organ failure risk
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Move person to cool, shaded area; Remove excess clothing; Cool rapidly: apply cool water to skin, fan the person, apply ice packs to neck, armpits, and groin; If conscious, offer cool water to sip; Do NOT give fever-reducing medicines; Monitor breathing and consciousness
Medicines: Ice packs, Cool water, Cooling blanket, IV fluids (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — heat stroke is a medical emergency with potential for organ damage
Warnings: Do NOT give Paracetamol or Ibuprofen for heat stroke — they are ineffective and may worsen organ damage. Core body temperature above 40°C (104°F) is a medical emergency. Rapid cooling is the priority.

Symptoms: heavy sweating cool pale clammy skin rapid weak pulse nausea vomiting muscle cramps weakness dizziness headache fainting after prolonged heat exposure dehydration salt loss
FirstAidSteps: Move person to cool, shaded environment; Lay down with legs elevated; Remove excess clothing; Give cool water or sports drinks (if conscious); Apply cool, wet cloths to skin; Fan the person; Seek medical attention if symptoms worsen
Medicines: Cool water / sports drinks (electrolytes), Cool compresses, ORS
EmergencyTriggers: Symptoms worsen or do not improve within 30 minutes, Person becomes confused or loses consciousness, Suspected heat stroke
Warnings: Heat exhaustion can progress to heat stroke — monitor closely. Avoid alcohol, caffeine, and sugary drinks. Rest and cool environment are key.

Symptoms: core body temperature below 35 degrees shivering intense cold skin pale blue lips confusion slurred speech stumbling loss of coordination drowsiness rigid muscles weak pulse slow breathing
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services; Move to warm, dry environment; Remove wet clothing; Warm gradually — use blankets, warm (not hot) drinks if conscious, body heat; Apply warm (not hot) compresses to armpits, groin, and neck; Do NOT rub skin vigorously — can cause cardiac arrhythmia; If unconscious, begin CPR if needed
Medicines: Warm blankets, Warm (not hot) drinks for conscious patients, IV warm fluids (hospital), No specific OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always for severe hypothermia (temperature below 32°C/90°F), If unconscious or shivering stops, Suspected cardiac arrhythmia
Warnings: Do NOT rub or massage cold limbs — can trigger dangerous heart rhythms. Do NOT give alcohol. Rewarming too fast can cause 'rewarming shock'. Even after rescue, heart may still be at risk.

Symptoms: frozen white or grayish-yellow skin hard numb cold extremities waxy appearance fingers toes ears nose affected pins and needles tingling before numbness blistering after rewarming
FirstAidSteps: Move to warm environment; Remove wet clothing and jewelry; Do NOT walk on frostbitten feet; Rewarm affected areas in warm (not hot) water (37–40°C/99–104°F) for 15–30 minutes; Do NOT rub frostbitten areas; Apply loose sterile dressing after rewarming; Give pain relief as rewarming is painful
Medicines: Ibuprofen (reduces inflammation), Sterile gauze dressings, Warm water for rewarming
EmergencyTriggers: Always for severe frostbite (blisters, blackened tissue, loss of sensation), Suspected deep frostbite
Warnings: Do NOT rewarm if there is a risk of refreezing — thaw-refreeze cycle causes more damage. Do NOT rub or massage. Do NOT break blisters. Alcohol and smoking worsen frostbite — avoid.

Symptoms: severe burning eye pain chemical splash to eye redness tearing involuntary blinking inability to open eye corneal damage blurred vision after chemical acid alkali contact
FirstAidSteps: Flush the eye immediately with large amounts of clean water or saline for at least 20–30 minutes; Hold eye open during irrigation; Remove contact lenses before or during flushing; Call emergency services or go to emergency room; Do NOT rub the eye; Keep irrigating on the way to hospital
Medicines: Sterile eye wash / saline, Eye irrigation bottle
EmergencyTriggers: Always — chemical eye injuries are emergencies
Warnings: Time is critical — immediate flushing significantly improves outcomes. Alkaline burns (e.g., cement, oven cleaner) are more serious than acid burns and require longer irrigation.

Symptoms: sensation of something in eye excessive tearing redness pain when blinking scratchy feeling foreign body dust metal glass splinter visible on eye surface blurred vision
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT rub the eye; Try to blink rapidly — tears may flush out the object; Gently pull upper eyelid over lower eyelid; Flush with clean water or sterile saline; If object is on white of eye and visible, try gently removing with corner of damp clean cloth; If object is on cornea or embedded — cover eye loosely and go to hospital
Medicines: Sterile eye wash / saline, Eye irrigation
EmergencyTriggers: Object is embedded in the eye, Object is on the cornea, Severe pain or vision changes, Chemical or metal splash
Warnings: Do NOT attempt to remove embedded objects — cover the eye and seek medical help. Do NOT rub the eye. Metal fragments from grinding may cause rust rings requiring surgical removal.

Symptoms: sudden loss of consciousness rhythmic jerking movements tonic-clonic muscle rigidity uncontrolled shaking eye rolling frothing at mouth tongue biting bladder loss postictal confusion after seizure
FirstAidSteps: Keep calm; Clear the area of hard/sharp objects to prevent injury; Cushion the head with something soft; Place person on their side (recovery position) to prevent choking on saliva; Time the seizure; Do NOT restrain the person or put anything in their mouth; Stay with them until fully conscious; When convulsions stop, reassure the person calmly
Medicines: Rectal diazepam (if prescribed for known epilepsy), Buccal midazolam (prescribed), No OTC medicine for seizures
EmergencyTriggers: First ever seizure, Seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, Multiple seizures without recovery between them, Seizure in water, Injury occurs during seizure, Person is pregnant, Person has diabetes
Warnings: Do NOT put anything in the person's mouth — people cannot swallow their tongue during seizures. Do NOT restrain. After seizure, person may be confused (postictal state) — this is normal.

Symptoms: intense localized bone pain swelling bruising deformity crepitus grating sensation inability to move limb abnormal position limb shortening visible bone open fracture tenderness on palpation
FirstAidSteps: Keep the person still; Do NOT try to straighten the bone; Immobilize the injured area with a splint or sling (using rolled newspaper, cardboard, or clothing); Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling; Elevate the injured limb if possible; Treat for shock if needed (lay flat, keep warm); Seek emergency medical care
Medicines: Ice pack, Splint/sling material, Paracetamol or Ibuprofen (for pain), Triangular bandage
EmergencyTriggers: Suspected fracture of spine, pelvis, or thigh, Open fracture (bone visible through skin), Significant deformity, Numbness or loss of circulation below fracture, Multiple fractures
Warnings: Do NOT move a person with suspected spinal injury unless in immediate danger. Open fractures carry high risk of infection. Do NOT attempt to push bone back in.

Symptoms: joint pain swelling bruising around joint limited range of motion tenderness on touch instability of joint muscle pull overstretching ligament damage ankle wrist knee sprain
FirstAidSteps: Follow PRICE: Protect, Rest, Ice, Compression, Elevation; Rest the injured area — avoid activity; Apply ice pack (wrapped in cloth) for 15–20 minutes every 2 hours; Apply compression bandage — not too tight; Elevate the limb above heart level; Take pain relief if needed; Seek medical care if pain is severe or no improvement in 24–48 hours
Medicines: Ice pack, Compression bandage, Paracetamol or Ibuprofen, Topical pain-relief gel (Diclofenac gel)
EmergencyTriggers: Significant swelling or deformity (may indicate fracture), Inability to bear weight, Severe pain, Numbness or tingling
Warnings: Applying ice directly to skin causes ice burns — always wrap in cloth. Do NOT apply heat in the first 48 hours. PRICE method is the standard for sprains.

Symptoms: visible joint deformity intense pain inability to move joint swelling bruising joint out of normal position shoulder hip elbow kneecap displaced locked joint numbness below dislocation
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services or go to emergency room; Immobilize the joint in the position found — do NOT attempt to relocate; Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth; Support the dislocated limb with padding/pillows; Monitor circulation: check pulse, sensation, and color below the dislocation; Treat for shock if needed
Medicines: Ice pack, Immobilization support, Pain relief (hospital-administered), NSAIDs after reduction
EmergencyTriggers: Always — dislocation requires professional medical reduction
Warnings: NEVER attempt to reduce (put back) a dislocation yourself — risk of nerve, blood vessel, and bone damage. Loss of sensation or circulation is an emergency.

Symptoms: severe back pain neck pain after trauma numbness tingling in limbs weakness paralysis loss of bladder or bowel control tenderness along spine pain radiating down legs
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Do NOT move the person unless there is immediate danger (fire, drowning); Keep the person still — tell them not to move; Support head and neck in position found; If movement is essential, use log-roll technique (at least 3 people); Monitor breathing; Begin CPR if needed without moving the spine
Medicines: Cervical collar (if trained), Spine board (emergency services)
EmergencyTriggers: Always if spinal injury is suspected
Warnings: Spinal injury can cause permanent paralysis if mishandled. Never move a suspected spinal injury victim unless absolutely necessary for safety. Do NOT remove a motorcycle helmet if spinal injury is suspected.

Symptoms: nausea vomiting abdominal cramps diarrhea drooling altered consciousness confusion dizziness burns around mouth chemical taste on lips unknown substance swallowed household product medication overdose plant ingestion
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services or Poison Control Center immediately; Keep the person calm and conscious; If person vomits, prevent choking by turning on side; Identify the substance if possible — keep the container; Do NOT give anything to eat/drink unless advised by Poison Control; Do NOT induce vomiting unless specifically instructed by Poison Control
Medicines: Activated charcoal (hospital/Poison Control directed only), Specific antidotes (hospital), No OTC self-treatment
EmergencyTriggers: Always — call Poison Control or emergency services immediately
Warnings: Do NOT induce vomiting — it can cause further damage with corrosives and increases risk of aspiration. Bring the poison container/packaging to the hospital. Keep Poison Control numbers accessible.

Symptoms: headache dizziness nausea after fume exposure coughing burning throat difficulty breathing confusion loss of consciousness chemical smell gas exposure carbon monoxide toxic fumes
FirstAidSteps: Move person to fresh air immediately — ensure your own safety first; Call emergency services; Loosen tight clothing; If breathing stops, begin CPR; Do NOT enter a confined space with fumes without protection; Identify the gas if possible
Medicines: Oxygen (emergency services), CPR equipment, No OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always — inhaled poisoning is always an emergency
Warnings: Carbon monoxide has no color or smell — install CO detectors at home. Rescuers can also become victims — do NOT enter enclosed spaces with toxic gases without proper protection.

Symptoms: redness burning pain blistering skin discoloration chemical contact dermatitis rash swelling after touching toxic substance pesticide corrosive acid alkali splash skin irritation
FirstAidSteps: Remove the person from the source; Remove contaminated clothing and jewelry (wear gloves); Flush skin with large amounts of water for 15–20 minutes; Call Poison Control or emergency services; Do NOT rub the skin
Medicines: Water for irrigation, Sterile dressings
EmergencyTriggers: If symptoms develop (burning, blistering, breathing difficulty), Always for pesticide/chemical exposure
Warnings: Do NOT use neutralizing agents unless directed by Poison Control. Wear gloves when assisting to avoid self-contamination.

Symptoms: unresponsive slow shallow breathing pinpoint pupils blue lips opioid signs confusion agitation hallucination rapid heart rate drug misuse intentional or accidental excessive medication intake
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Keep the person awake and conscious if possible; Place in recovery position if unconscious; Monitor breathing — begin CPR if breathing stops; Do NOT leave the person alone; Gather information on what was taken, how much, and when
Medicines: Naloxone / Narcan (for opioid overdose — nasal spray or injection), CPR equipment, Activated charcoal (hospital), No other OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always — all drug overdoses require emergency medical attention
Warnings: For opioid overdose: administer Naloxone if available — it can reverse overdose temporarily. The person will still need emergency care. Do NOT induce vomiting. Stay with the person.

Symptoms: sudden shortness of breath wheezing tightness in chest coughing inability to complete sentences rapid breathing accessory muscle use intercostal recession silent chest severe attack blue lips
FirstAidSteps: Sit the person upright and forward — do NOT lay them down; Encourage calm, slow breathing; Give 1 puff of reliever inhaler (blue — Salbutamol/Albuterol) immediately; Give one puff every 60 seconds, up to 10 puffs; If no improvement after 10 puffs — call emergency services and repeat every 10 minutes; Keep the person calm and warm
Medicines: Salbutamol (Albuterol) reliever inhaler (blue), Ipratropium bromide inhaler (hospital), Oral corticosteroids (Prednisolone — hospital), Spacer device for inhaler
EmergencyTriggers: No improvement after 10 puffs of reliever inhaler, Lips or fingernails turning blue, Person is exhausted or cannot speak, This is the first ever asthma attack
Warnings: Never lay an asthma patient down. Spacer devices make inhalers more effective. Always have reliever inhalers accessible. Do NOT use preventer inhalers (brown/purple) during acute attacks.

Symptoms: labored breathing shortness of breath rapid shallow respirations chest tightness inability to speak in full sentences accessory muscle use flared nostrils cyanosis gasping air hunger
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Sit the person upright in a position of comfort; Loosen tight clothing around neck and chest; Keep the person calm; If person has prescribed inhaler, help them use it; Begin CPR if breathing stops
Medicines: Reliever inhaler (if asthma), Oxygen (emergency services), No OTC medicines for unknown breathing difficulty
EmergencyTriggers: Always for unexplained severe difficulty breathing
Warnings: Do NOT give the person water or food. Determine cause if possible (asthma, allergic reaction, heart failure, panic attack). Blueness of lips/fingers indicates oxygen deprivation — emergency.

Symptoms: sudden loss of consciousness brief episode of collapse sudden weakness dizziness before faint tunnel vision pale sweating nausea before passing out slow recovery quick return of consciousness
FirstAidSteps: Catch the person if possible to prevent injury from falling; Lay the person flat and raise legs 30 cm (12 inches) above heart level; Loosen tight clothing (collar, belt); Ensure fresh air — open windows if indoors; Do NOT give anything to eat/drink until fully conscious; Once conscious, have person sit up slowly; Offer water after they are fully alert
Medicines: No medicines required for simple faint, Smelling salts (use cautiously — not for everyone), Glucose drink once fully conscious (if diabetic)
EmergencyTriggers: Does not regain consciousness within 1–2 minutes, Fainting after head injury, Fainting with chest pain or palpitations, Fainting in water, Pregnant woman faints
Warnings: Fainting in elderly or people with heart conditions can be serious. Do NOT slap or splash water on the face. Raising legs is the priority. Investigate underlying cause if fainting recurs.

Symptoms: pale cold clammy skin rapid weak pulse confusion agitation rapid shallow breathing extreme weakness decreased urine output dizziness low blood pressure nausea following major injury blood loss allergy infection
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Lay the person flat — raise legs about 30 cm unless head/spinal/leg injury; Keep the person warm with a blanket; Do NOT give food or drink; Loosen tight clothing; Monitor breathing and consciousness; Begin CPR if needed; Treat the underlying cause (control bleeding, etc.)
Medicines: IV fluids (hospital), Vasopressors (hospital), No OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always — shock is always a medical emergency
Warnings: Shock types: hypovolemic (blood loss), anaphylactic (allergy), cardiogenic (heart), septic (infection), neurogenic (spinal). Do NOT give food/fluids by mouth. Shock can be fatal — rapid treatment is essential.

Symptoms: blood draining from ear canal after head trauma ear pain hearing loss possible skull fracture sign fluid behind eardrum tinnitus ringing in ear head injury related bleeding
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT insert anything into the ear canal; Do NOT try to stop the bleeding by blocking the ear; Tilt the person's head so the affected ear is down — allow blood to drain; Place sterile gauze loosely over the ear; Call emergency services — this may indicate skull fracture
Medicines: Sterile gauze (covering only)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — bleeding from ear after head trauma indicates possible skull fracture
Warnings: Bleeding from ear after head injury is a sign of serious skull fracture. Do NOT plug the ear — allowing blood to drain prevents pressure buildup. This is always an emergency.

Symptoms: headache confusion memory loss amnesia dizziness nausea vomiting sensitivity to light and noise feeling foggy slowed reaction repeated questioning disorientation after head impact loss of consciousness
FirstAidSteps: Keep the person still and calm; Apply cold compress to any swelling; Monitor for worsening symptoms: confusion, vomiting, unequal pupils, seizures; Do NOT leave person alone for 24 hours; Wake the person every 2 hours overnight to check consciousness; Seek medical evaluation
Medicines: Ice pack (for swelling), Paracetamol for headache (NOT Ibuprofen or Aspirin — may increase bleeding risk), Do NOT give sedating medications
EmergencyTriggers: Loss of consciousness, Repeated vomiting, Severe headache, Confusion, One pupil larger than other, Clear fluid from nose/ear, Weakness or numbness
Warnings: Do NOT give NSAIDs (Ibuprofen, Aspirin) for head injury — risk of increased bleeding. Always seek medical evaluation after any significant head injury. Do NOT allow sleep immediately after concussion without monitoring.

Symptoms: throbbing tooth pain sharp pain when biting or chewing sensitivity to hot or cold sweet foods swollen gums abscess pus fever associated with tooth infection jaw pain referred pain
FirstAidSteps: Rinse mouth with warm salt water; Use dental floss to remove any trapped food; Apply clove oil to affected area (natural pain relief); Place a cold compress on the cheek; Take over-the-counter pain reliever; See a dentist as soon as possible
Medicines: Paracetamol or Ibuprofen (pain relief), Clove oil (topical numbing), Benzocaine gel (Orajel — topical), Salt water rinse
EmergencyTriggers: Swelling spreading to face/neck, Difficulty breathing or swallowing, High fever, Pus or abscess visible
Warnings: Dental abscess with spreading swelling can be life-threatening — seek emergency care. Do NOT place aspirin directly on the gum — causes chemical burn. Root cause must be treated by a dentist.

Symptoms: tooth completely out of socket adult permanent tooth missing bleeding socket gum pain trauma to mouth impact injury tooth in hand or on ground time-sensitive dental emergency
FirstAidSteps: Find the tooth and pick up by the crown (white part) — do NOT touch the root; If dirty, rinse gently with milk or saline — do NOT scrub; Try to reinsert in the socket immediately if adult tooth; If cannot reinsert, store in milk, saline, or inside the cheek; See a dentist IMMEDIATELY — ideally within 30 minutes
Medicines: Saline solution or milk (for tooth storage), Paracetamol (pain relief)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — go to a dentist or emergency room immediately
Warnings: Time is critical — tooth can be reimplanted successfully if treated within 30–60 minutes. Do NOT store tooth in water. Baby teeth should NOT be reimplanted. Do NOT rub or scrub the tooth root.

Symptoms: immediate burning stinging pain red welts raised linear marks on skin tentacle marks itching throbbing localized swelling possible nausea dizziness after marine contact box jellyfish anaphylaxis risk
FirstAidSteps: Get out of the water; Do NOT rub the affected area; Rinse with seawater (NOT fresh water); Remove visible tentacles with tweezers or card — do NOT use bare hands; Immerse in hot water (as hot as tolerable) or apply heat pack for 20 minutes — this deactivates venom; Apply antihistamine cream; Take oral antihistamine and pain reliever
Medicines: Antihistamine cream (Hydrocortisone 1%), Oral antihistamine (Cetirizine), Ibuprofen or Paracetamol, Heat pack / hot water soak
EmergencyTriggers: Signs of anaphylaxis, Sting on face/genitals, Systemic symptoms (chest pain, difficulty breathing), Box jellyfish sting (highly venomous — emergency)
Warnings: Do NOT urinate on the sting — this is a myth and may worsen the reaction. Do NOT use fresh water or vinegar for most jellyfish. Box jellyfish stings may require vinegar — seek local guidance. Do NOT rub tentacles off.

Symptoms: red hot painful skin after sun exposure tender to touch skin peeling after 24 to 48 hours blistering in severe cases chills fever nausea headache UV radiation overexposure
FirstAidSteps: Move to shade or indoors; Cool the skin with cool (not cold) water or damp cloths; Take a cool bath or shower; Apply moisturizing lotion or aloe vera gel; Drink plenty of fluids; Take pain reliever if needed; Avoid further sun exposure until healed; Do NOT pop blisters
Medicines: Aloe vera gel, Moisturizing cream/lotion, Ibuprofen or Paracetamol, After-sun lotion, Hydrocortisone cream 1% (for inflammation)
EmergencyTriggers: Blistering over large area, Fever above 38.5°C (101.3°F), Extreme pain, Confusion or dehydration signs
Warnings: Do NOT use butter, oil, or petroleum jelly. Sunburns can increase skin cancer risk over time. Blistered sunburns need medical evaluation. Protect with broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen.

Symptoms: sudden intense fear chest tightness pounding heart racing heartbeat shortness of breath trembling shaking sweating dizziness numbness tingling feeling of unreality fear of dying overwhelming anxiety no physical trigger
FirstAidSteps: Move to a calm, quiet environment; Stay with the person — speak calmly and reassuringly; Guide the person in slow breathing: breathe in for 4 counts, hold for 4, out for; Help them focus on 5 things they can see, 4 they can touch, 3 they can hear; Reassure them the attack will pass and is not physically dangerous; Do NOT leave them alone
Medicines: No medicines required in most cases, Prescribed benzodiazepines (e.g., Diazepam — if already prescribed), Lavender aromatherapy (calming support)
EmergencyTriggers: First ever attack (to rule out cardiac event), Chest pain persisting after attack, Person requests medical help
Warnings: Panic attacks, while terrifying, are not physically dangerous. Breathing techniques and grounding exercises are the most effective first-line interventions. Hyperventilation during panic attacks can cause tingling — slow breathing corrects CO2 levels.

Symptoms: nose pain after direct impact swelling bruising under eyes nasal bleeding deviation from normal position difficulty breathing through nose crepitus on touch tenderness nasal bridge facial trauma
FirstAidSteps: Tilt head slightly forward and breathe through the mouth; Pinch nose and apply pressure to stop bleeding; Apply ice pack wrapped in cloth to reduce swelling; Do NOT try to straighten the nose; Seek medical attention
Medicines: Ice pack, Paracetamol (pain relief)
EmergencyTriggers: Breathing difficulty, Heavy bleeding that won't stop, Clear fluid from nose (may indicate skull fracture), Swelling of the eye (orbit fracture)
Warnings: Do NOT try to reset the nose yourself. Clear fluid from nose after injury may indicate a serious skull base fracture — emergency.

Situation: Ear Injury / Foreign Object in Ear
Symptoms: ear pain sensation of fullness in ear muffled hearing buzzing insect in ear object visible or felt in ear canal discharge from ear child inserted object hearing loss
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT probe the ear with cotton buds or objects; Tilt the head with affected ear downward — gravity may dislodge object; For insects: shine a light into the ear, or flush with warm water; Seek medical attention for removal
Medicines: Warm water (for flushing — water-based objects), No OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Pain, hearing loss, or object cannot be removed, Live insect in ear
Warnings: Do NOT insert objects into the ear. Attempting to remove a foreign object without visualization can push it deeper and damage the eardrum. Seek medical assistance for professional removal.

Symptoms: itchy watery eyes redness swollen eyelids burning sensation discharge seasonal allergy pollen dust pet dander trigger bilateral eye irritation rubbing eyes mucus discharge conjunctivitis-like symptoms
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT rub the eyes; Flush with sterile saline or clean water; Apply cold compress over closed eyes; Remove contact lenses; Take oral antihistamine
Medicines: Antihistamine eye drops (Ketotifen / Azelastine), Oral antihistamine (Cetirizine), Sterile saline eye drops (preservative-free), Mast cell stabilizer eye drops
EmergencyTriggers: Severe eye pain, Loss of vision, Redness with pus (may indicate infection)
Warnings: Do NOT rub eyes — causes mast cell degranulation and worsens allergic reaction. Remove contact lenses during treatment.

Symptoms: extreme cold intolerance decreased consciousness hypothermia severe fatigue confusion bradycardia slow heart rate swollen face puffiness low sodium levels respiratory depression life-threatening thyroid emergency
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Keep person warm — they cannot regulate body temperature; Position on side if unconscious; Begin CPR if needed; Provide emergency information on their medical condition to responders
Medicines: IV thyroid hormone replacement (hospital only), IV corticosteroids (hospital), IV fluids (hospital)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — this is a life-threatening emergency
Warnings: This is a hospital-only emergency requiring IV thyroid hormone. Lay person with hypothermia precautions. Do NOT rewarm too rapidly.

Symptoms: vaginal bleeding during pregnancy abdominal cramping lower back pain passing tissue or clots loss of pregnancy symptoms sudden decrease in nausea cervix open on examination early pregnancy loss
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services or go to hospital immediately; Keep the person calm and reassured; Help them lie down and rest; Offer emotional support; Do NOT leave the person alone; Collect any passed tissue in a clean container for medical evaluation
Medicines: No OTC medicines — hospital evaluation required, Paracetamol (for pain only — consult doctor)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — all suspected miscarriage requires urgent medical evaluation
Warnings: Do NOT attempt to intervene physically. Emotional support is critical. Emergency care is required to manage bleeding and assess whether the miscarriage is complete.

Symptoms: high blood pressure in pregnancy severe headache visual disturbances spots flashing lights upper abdominal pain right upper quadrant swelling of face hands feet protein in urine third trimester
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Keep the woman calm and lying on her left side; Monitor for seizures (eclampsia); Do NOT give any medication unless prescribed; Keep environment quiet and low-stimulation
Medicines: No OTC medicines — hospital treatment required (Magnesium sulfate, antihypertensives)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — pre-eclampsia is a medical emergency
Warnings: Symptoms: high blood pressure, severe headache, visual disturbances, swelling, upper abdominal pain. Left lateral position improves blood flow to fetus. Eclamptic seizures can be fatal.

Symptoms: blood dripping from front of nose one or both nostrils spontaneous bleeding after nose-picking dry air irritation anticoagulant use high blood pressure nasal trauma recent cold or infection
FirstAidSteps: Sit upright — lean slightly forward; Pinch the soft part of the nose firmly; Breathe through mouth; Hold for 10–15 minutes continuously; Apply cold compress to bridge of nose; After bleeding stops, avoid nose-blowing for several hours
Medicines: Nasal decongestant spray (short-term), Saline nasal spray (prevents dryness), Petroleum jelly applied inside nostrils (prevents recurrence)
EmergencyTriggers: Bleeding does not stop after 20–30 minutes, Caused by head injury, Very heavy blood loss, Blood-thinning medication use
Warnings: Most nosebleeds are minor and can be managed at home. Tilt head FORWARD — not backward.

Symptoms: brief electrical shock tingling sensation in hand arm muscle jerk visible burn mark at contact point mild confusion momentary loss of consciousness entry exit wound possible minor cardiac irregularity
FirstAidSteps: Do NOT touch the person if still in contact with electricity; Turn off power at source; If person is conscious and appears uninjured, reassure them; Check for burns at entry and exit points; Seek medical attention — internal injuries may not be visible; Monitor for irregular heartbeat
Medicines: Ice pack for minor burns, Sterile dressings
EmergencyTriggers: Always seek medical evaluation after any electric shock — even if minor
Warnings: Electricity can cause internal burns not visible externally. Cardiac arrhythmias can develop after electric shock. Always go to hospital.

Symptoms: throbbing headache dizziness nausea confusion weakness shortness of breath loss of consciousness cherry red lips skin multiple people in same room affected no odor colorless gas silent killer
FirstAidSteps: Get everyone out of the building immediately; Call emergency services; Do NOT re-enter the building; Provide fresh air; Begin CPR if needed; Hospital will provide high-concentration oxygen treatment
Medicines: High-flow oxygen (emergency services / hospital), Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (hospital, if severe)
EmergencyTriggers: Always — carbon monoxide poisoning is always an emergency
Warnings: CO is odorless and colorless — install CO detectors. Symptoms include headache, dizziness, confusion, nausea. A CO detector is essential in all homes with gas appliances.

Symptoms: wound on foot in diabetic patient slow healing redness warmth swelling pus or discharge numbness or reduced sensation in foot ulcer development gangrene risk peripheral neuropathy poor circulation
FirstAidSteps: Clean wound gently with saline or mild antiseptic; Cover with sterile dressing; Do NOT ignore even minor wounds on diabetic feet; Seek medical attention promptly; Avoid walking on the wound
Medicines: Sterile saline, Non-adhesive sterile dressings, Antiseptic solution, Antibiotics (prescription)
EmergencyTriggers: All diabetic foot wounds require prompt medical evaluation
Warnings: Diabetic foot wounds can progress rapidly to serious infection and amputation. Poor circulation and neuropathy mean wounds may not be painful. Never delay medical care for diabetic foot injuries.

Symptoms: shivering cold skin temperature between 32 and 35 degrees Celsius slurred speech clumsiness poor coordination numbness in extremities pallor fatigue confusion after cold exposure wet clothing
FirstAidSteps: Move to a warm, sheltered area; Replace wet clothing with dry; Wrap in blankets or warm clothing; Give warm (not hot) drinks if conscious; Apply chemical heat packs wrapped in cloth to armpits and groin; Monitor temperature and consciousness
Medicines: Thermal blanket, Warm fluids (non-alcoholic), Chemical heat packs
EmergencyTriggers: Temperature does not improve, Loss of consciousness, Temperature below 32°C (90°F)
Warnings: Do NOT give alcohol — it causes peripheral vasodilation and increases heat loss. Gradual rewarming is safer than rapid rewarming.

Symptoms: burning pain on urination frequent urge to urinate cloudy dark urine strong foul odor lower abdominal pain pelvic pressure blood in urine hematuria low-grade fever back pain kidney involvement
FirstAidSteps: Encourage increased fluid intake — especially water; Urge frequent urination; Apply warm compress to lower abdomen for comfort; Avoid caffeine, alcohol, and spicy foods; Seek medical attention for antibiotic prescription; Cranberry products may help prevent but not treat UTI
Medicines: Antibiotics (prescription only — Trimethoprim, Nitrofurantoin, Amoxicillin), Phenazopyridine (urinary pain relief — OTC in some regions), Paracetamol (pain/fever), Plenty of water
EmergencyTriggers: Fever above 38°C, Lower back or flank pain (kidney involvement), Blood in urine, Symptoms in men or children, Pregnant women with UTI symptoms
Warnings: UTIs require antibiotics — self-treatment with non-prescription medicines only manages symptoms. Delay in treatment of upper UTI (pyelonephritis) can lead to kidney damage or sepsis.

Symptoms: sudden chest pain pressure tightness squeezing sensation shortness of breath sweating nausea radiating arm jaw back unknown cardiac or respiratory cause requires immediate emergency evaluation
FirstAidSteps: Call emergency services immediately; Have the person sit or lie in comfortable position; Loosen tight clothing; If suspected heart attack: give 300mg Aspirin to chew if not allergic; Monitor breathing and consciousness; Begin CPR if they stop breathing; Do NOT leave the person alone
Medicines: Aspirin 300mg (if suspected heart attack, no allergy), GTN spray (if prescribed), No other OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always — all unexplained chest pain requires emergency evaluation
Warnings: Never assume chest pain is minor. Causes include heart attack, pulmonary embolism, aortic dissection, pneumothorax — all potentially fatal. Treat all chest pain as a possible cardiac event until proven otherwise.

Symptoms: coughing from smoke inhalation hoarse voice sore throat singed nasal hairs soot around nose mouth shortness of breath chest tightness confusion headache carbon monoxide exposure fire victim
FirstAidSteps: Move to fresh air immediately; Call emergency services; Loosen tight clothing; If conscious: encourage slow, calm breathing; If unconscious: recovery position and begin CPR if needed; Seek emergency medical care even if symptoms seem mild
Medicines: Oxygen (emergency services), IV fluids (hospital), No OTC medicines
EmergencyTriggers: Always — smoke inhalation can cause delayed respiratory failure
Warnings: Symptoms may worsen hours after exposure. Carbon monoxide poisoning from smoke is common. Hospital evaluation is essential even if person initially appears fine.
